currentUse: Spanish-language Protestant congregation — Iglesia Cristiana Pacto de Fe Inc., listed at 5408 S 32nd St, Omaha, NE 68107, on the former St. Anthony parish property (probable; see gaps on the 5402 vs 5408 address point)
summary: St. Anthony (Šv. Antano) Lithuanian parish at 5402 S. 32nd Street in South Omaha closed in June 2014 as part of an Archdiocese of Omaha restructuring: Archbishop George J. Lucas presided at a June 14 Mass, followed on June 15 by a Eucharistic procession to Ss. Peter and Paul Church, the parish it was merged into, with the building to remain available for funerals and weddings "until another use is made of the properties." A widely cited photo caption records that the church was subsequently sold to an Omaha-based investor group for potential sale or lease to a Christian denomination. Today the 1936 red-brick church property is occupied by Iglesia Cristiana Pacto de Fe Inc., a Spanish-language Protestant congregation (pastors Marco Tulio Ortiz and Sayra Ortiz) listed at 5408 S 32nd St — the church-type parcel on that block — with an active Google Maps presence and directory listings updated as recently as June 2026, so the building stands and is in weekly religious use. The former parish school closed in 1980 and its building has been described as abandoned, while the church cornerstone retains its Lithuanian inscription. The successor parish, Sts. Peter and Paul, still describes itself as celebrating Lithuanian, Croatian, Slovenian and Hungarian heritage. Omaha's Lithuanian community remains visible outside the parish through the Lithuanian Bakery (since 1963) and the Lithuanian "Path of the Sun" sculpture garden at Lauritzen Gardens (2015-2017), and the Douglas County Historical Society mounted a feature on the community in November 2021.

developments.headline: Archdiocese announces final liturgies; St. Anthony merged into Ss. Peter and Paul
developments.detail: The Catholic Voice (Archdiocese of Omaha) reported that St. Anthony, founded 1907, was merged with Ss. Peter and Paul Parish; Archbishop George J. Lucas presided at a June 14, 2014 Mass at St. Anthony, followed by a June 15 Eucharistic procession to Ss. Peter and Paul. Closing churches were to remain available for funerals and weddings until another use was made of the properties.

sources.title: Three Omaha churches prepare for last liturgies

developments.headline: Church reported sold to Omaha-based investor group
developments.detail: A photographer's documented caption on a photo of the church states it "has since been sold to an Omaha-based investor group for potential sale or lease to a Christian denomination." No buyer name, price, or sale date is published in any accessible source found.

sources.title: St. Anthony's Catholic Church - Omaha, NE (photo description)
sources.date: photo posted 2012; caption updated after 2014 closure

developments.headline: Former parish property occupied by Iglesia Cristiana Pacto de Fe, a Spanish-language congregation
developments.detail: Iglesia Cristiana Pacto de Fe Inc. (EIN 27-1297416), pastored by Marco Tulio Ortiz and Sayra Ortiz, is listed at 5408 S 32nd St, Omaha NE 68107 — the church-type parcel on the former St. Anthony block — with an active Google Maps listing (open daily, photos showing a red-brick raised-basement church consistent with the 1936 St. Anthony building) and a directory entry updated June 2026.

IGLESIA CRISTIANA PACTO DE FE INC (EIN 27-1297416)

developments.headline: Douglas County Historical Society features Omaha's Lithuanian community
developments.detail: A historical society feature recounted the parish's founding (1906/consecrated 1907), its sponsorship of post-WWII Lithuanian refugees under Rev. Joseph Jusevicius (temporary housing in the church basement, job placement), and noted the community "continues its mission to keep traditions alive" seventy years after postwar immigration.

developments.headline: School building abandoned; Lithuanian heritage persists elsewhere in Omaha
developments.detail: Global True Lithuania documents that the parish school closed in 1980 and the school building is now abandoned; only the church itself bears a Lithuanian cornerstone inscription. Living heritage sites include the Lithuanian Bakery (since 1963) and the Lithuanian "Path of the Sun" sculpture garden at Lauritzen Gardens (created 2015-2017).

sources.publisher: Sts. Peter & Paul Catholic Church, Omaha

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions; several refinements. (1) Archive founding date 1907: Catholic Voice also says founded 1907, Douglas County Historical Society says founded 1906/consecrated 1907, while Wikipedia's archdiocese list says "founded in 1901 for Lithuanian immigrants" — consistent with the archive's note that the Šv. Antano draugija predates the church (1900/01). The standing building is not the 1907 church: it was built in 1936 after the original burned (Global True Lithuania, Flickr). (2) Archive "closed/merged 2014 into Sts. Peter and Paul" — confirmed exactly (final Mass June 14, 2014, Archbishop Lucas; procession to Ss. Peter and Paul June 15). (3) Archive "sold to Spanish-speakers" — refined: a photo caption records an intermediate sale to an Omaha-based investor group for sale/lease to a Christian denomination; the congregation now on the property, Iglesia Cristiana Pacto de Fe, is indeed Spanish-speaking but Protestant/evangelical, not Catholic. (4) The 2014 closure was not immediate abandonment — the church initially remained available for occasional Masses, feasts, funerals and weddings.
gaps: Sale specifics are unpublished in accessible sources: no buyer name, price, or deed date could be verified. Search snippets of a Zillow/Trulia record for "5408 S 32nd Street Church" show a July 11, 2017 sale for a nominal $2,000, but Zillow, Trulia, and the Douglas County Assessor all returned 403s, so this is unverified and excluded from the summary. The binding of Iglesia Cristiana Pacto de Fe to the St. Anthony sanctuary itself is probable, not verified by a text source: the historic church address is 5402 S 32nd St while the congregation lists 5408 S 32nd St (same block/parcel group; Google Maps photos show a matching red-brick raised-basement church, and the only church-type property record on the block is 5408). An Omaha World-Herald article "After merger, Omaha church auctions off nearly 100 years of history" (likely about the contents auction) is paywalled (HTTP 402) and could not be read; the fate of the parish's Lithuanian artifacts and furnishings is unknown. The Flickr caption reporting the investor-group sale is a single low-authority undated source. No press on preservation efforts, parish reunions, or the current condition of the abandoned school building was found. DiscoverMass still lists a stale "weekdays 10:00am — available for funerals of Lithuanian-American Community" entry that does not reflect current reality.
